enrichment.short_description: Open-source LLMOps platform for prompt engineering, evaluation, and observability of LLM applications.

long_form_blocks.heading: Overview
paragraphs: Agenta is an open-source LLMOps platform that brings prompt engineering, evaluation, and observability into one workflow for teams building applications on top of large language models. Its playground lets teams compare prompts and models side by side with full version history, so changes can be tracked and rolled back like code.

Beyond prompt management, Agenta provides automated evaluation (including LLM-as-a-judge and custom code evaluators), human evaluation workflows, and request tracing to catch regressions and failure points in production. The platform is model- and framework-agnostic, working with LangChain, LlamaIndex, the OpenAI SDK, and other stacks without locking teams into a single provider.

long_form_blocks.heading: Who It's For
paragraphs: Agenta is built for AI product teams — product managers, engineers, and domain experts — who need a shared place to iterate on prompts, run evaluations before shipping, and monitor LLM behavior once it's live. It can be self-hosted for full data control or used as a managed cloud service.

features.title: Prompt playground
features.description: Compare prompts and models side by side with complete version history and rollback.

features.title: Automated evaluation
features.description: LLM-as-a-judge, custom code evaluators, and structured test sets for regression testing prompts before release.

features.title: Human evaluation
features.description: Workflows for domain experts to manually review and score model outputs.

features.title: Observability and tracing
features.description: Request-level tracing to pinpoint failure points, with live performance monitoring and regression detection.

features.title: Framework-agnostic SDK
features.description: Works with LangChain, LlamaIndex, the OpenAI SDK, and other stacks without vendor lock-in.

features.title: Self-hosted or cloud deployment
features.description: Run Agenta on your own infrastructure or use the hosted cloud.agenta.ai service.

pros: Open-source core (MIT-licensed) that can be self-hosted for free
Combines prompt management, evaluation, and observability in one tool instead of stitching several together
Model- and framework-agnostic, avoiding vendor lock-in
Generous free Hobby tier for getting started
cons: Higher trace volumes and enterprise controls (SOC2, SSO, RBAC) require paid Pro/Business/Enterprise plans
Self-hosting requires managing your own infrastructure and updates
Newer platform compared to established LLM observability tools

faqs.question: Is Agenta open source?
faqs.answer: Yes. Agenta's core is MIT-licensed and can be self-hosted for free; the public repositories contain no closed-source components.

faqs.question: What's included in the free Hobby plan?
faqs.answer: 2 users, 5,000 traces per month, basic prompt management, up to 20 evaluations per month, and 30-day data retention with community support.

faqs.question: Can I use Agenta with any LLM provider?
faqs.answer: Yes, it's model- and framework-agnostic and integrates with LangChain, LlamaIndex, the OpenAI SDK, and other tooling.

faqs.question: How does Agenta pricing scale?
faqs.answer: Paid tiers (Pro at $49/month, Business at $399/month) add more users, traces, retention, and enterprise security features; extra traces beyond plan limits are billed at $5 per 10,000.

use_cases.title: Prompt engineering and versioning
use_cases.description: Iterate on and track prompt changes across models with a shared playground.

use_cases.title: Pre-release evaluation
use_cases.description: Run automated and human evaluations against test sets before deploying prompt or model changes.

use_cases.title: Production observability
use_cases.description: Trace live requests to debug failures and catch performance regressions.
use_cases.audience: Engineers operating LLM applications in production

enrichment.seo_meta_description: Agenta is an open-source LLMOps platform for prompt engineering, evaluation, and observability, self-hostable or available as a hosted service.

enrichment.short_description: Free, open-source AI gateway that routes requests to 20+ LLM providers through one OpenAI-compatible API.

long_form_blocks.heading: Overview
paragraphs: GoModel is an open-source AI gateway written in Go that sits in front of OpenAI, Anthropic, Gemini, Groq, xAI, Azure OpenAI, AWS Bedrock, and local runtimes like Ollama and vLLM, exposing them all through a single OpenAI-compatible endpoint. It's positioned as a lightweight alternative to gateways like LiteLLM, adding model aliasing, caching, usage tracking, and audit logging on top of routing.

The project ships as a small single binary or Docker image (around 16 MB, roughly 37 MB RAM at runtime) with an embedded admin dashboard, and can persist state to SQLite for zero-setup use or to PostgreSQL/MongoDB for production deployments.

long_form_blocks.heading: Who It's For
paragraphs: GoModel targets platform and infrastructure teams that need to centralize access to multiple LLM providers — for example, multi-tenant SaaS products billing AI usage back to customers, or teams wanting cost control through caching, failover, and per-user usage attribution.

features.title: Unified OpenAI-compatible API
features.description: One endpoint routes to 20+ providers including OpenAI, Anthropic, Gemini, Groq, xAI, Azure, Bedrock, and local Ollama/vLLM.

features.title: Model aliasing
features.description: Create stable names (e.g. "smart-chat") that map to underlying provider models, so backends can be swapped without code changes.

features.title: Request caching
features.description: Exact-match and semantic caching to cut down on redundant provider calls.

features.title: Usage and cost tracking
features.description: Token and dollar accounting per request, user path, and label.

features.title: Audit logging
features.description: Full request traceability, including resolved routes and provider attempts.

features.title: Failover and circuit breaking
features.description: Automatically routes around provider outages.

features.title: Lightweight self-hosted deployment
features.description: Runs as a small Docker image with an embedded admin dashboard; supports SQLite, PostgreSQL, or MongoDB storage.

pros: Fully free and open source under the MIT license, with no paid tiers for core functionality
Very small footprint — single binary, ~16 MB image, low memory use
Broad provider coverage including local inference runtimes
Built-in caching and failover can meaningfully cut API costs and downtime
cons: Newer project with a smaller community and track record than established gateways like LiteLLM
No managed cloud offering — self-hosting and operating it is on the user
Advanced governance features (per-workflow policies) require configuration effort

faqs.question: Is GoModel free?
faqs.answer: Yes. It's MIT-licensed and open source with no paid tiers for the core gateway.

faqs.question: What LLM providers does GoModel support?
faqs.answer: 20+ providers including OpenAI, Anthropic, Google Gemini, Groq, xAI, Azure OpenAI, AWS Bedrock, and local runtimes like Ollama and vLLM.

faqs.question: How is GoModel deployed?
faqs.answer: As a single lightweight Docker image with an embedded admin dashboard; storage can be SQLite, PostgreSQL, or MongoDB.

faqs.question: Is GoModel a drop-in replacement for LiteLLM?
faqs.answer: It's built as an alternative, exposing an OpenAI-compatible endpoint and comparable routing/caching features.

use_cases.title: Multi-provider LLM routing
use_cases.description: Give applications one stable API while switching or load-balancing across multiple LLM providers behind the scenes.

use_cases.title: Usage-based billing for AI features
use_cases.description: Track token and dollar usage per user or workflow to bill AI costs back to customers.

use_cases.title: Cost and reliability optimization
use_cases.description: Reduce redundant provider calls with caching and avoid outages with automatic failover.
use_cases.audience: Teams running LLM features in production

enrichment.seo_meta_description: GoModel is a free, open-source AI gateway written in Go that unifies 20+ LLM providers behind one self-hosted, OpenAI-compatible API.

enrichment.short_description: Open-source, self-hostable AI assistant that answers questions from your documents, the web, and custom agents.

long_form_blocks.heading: Overview
paragraphs: Khoj is an open-source, self-hostable AI assistant built to be understandable, hackable, and run under the user's own control. It uses retrieval-augmented generation to answer questions from a user's own files — PDFs, Markdown notes, GitHub repositories, and more — as well as from live web search.

Khoj can turn any online or local model (GPT, Claude, Gemini, Llama, Qwen, Mistral, and others) into a personal assistant, and supports building custom agents and scheduling recurring automations and research tasks. It's available as a free cloud app, a self-hosted deployment, or a hybrid of both.

long_form_blocks.heading: Who It's For
paragraphs: Khoj suits researchers, knowledge workers, and privacy-conscious individuals who want an AI assistant that can search their own documents and be self-hosted rather than relying entirely on a third-party cloud service.

features.title: Document search and RAG
features.description: Retrieves answers from a user's PDFs, Markdown notes, and GitHub repositories using retrieval-augmented generation.

features.title: Bring-your-own model
features.description: Works with cloud models (GPT, Claude, Gemini) or local/open models (Llama, Qwen, Mistral).

features.title: Custom agents
features.description: Build specialized assistants configured for specific tasks or knowledge domains.

features.title: Scheduled automations
features.description: Set up recurring research or task-tracking jobs the assistant runs automatically.

features.title: Web search integration
features.description: Pulls in live web results alongside personal document search.

features.title: Self-hostable
features.description: Runs entirely on a user's own machine or server for full data control, or via the hosted cloud app.

pros: Open source and self-hostable, giving full control over data and models
Works with a wide range of both cloud and local LLMs
Free tier and free self-hosting available to get started
Active open-source community and development on GitHub
cons: Higher upload limits and access to premium models require a paid cloud subscription
Self-hosting requires technical setup and infrastructure
Some advanced features (e.g. desktop research co-worker) are in beta

faqs.question: Is Khoj free?
faqs.answer: Yes, there is a free tier on the hosted cloud app, and self-hosting Khoj is free since it's open source.

faqs.question: Can I self-host Khoj?
faqs.answer: Yes. Khoj is open source and designed to run on your own hardware, in addition to the hosted cloud app.

faqs.question: What LLMs does Khoj support?
faqs.answer: Both cloud models (OpenAI, Anthropic, Gemini) and local/open models (Llama, Qwen, Mistral, and others).

faqs.question: Does Khoj work with my own documents?
faqs.answer: Yes, it uses retrieval-augmented generation to search and answer from uploaded PDFs, Markdown notes, and connected repositories.

use_cases.description: Ask natural-language questions over your own notes, PDFs, and repositories.

use_cases.title: Custom AI agents

use_cases.title: Privacy-first AI assistant
use_cases.description: Run the assistant entirely on your own infrastructure with local models.

enrichment.seo_meta_description: Khoj is an open-source, self-hostable AI assistant that searches your documents and the web, builds custom agents, and works with local or cloud LLMs.

enrichment.short_description: Open-source Kubernetes operator for deploying and autoscaling self-hosted LLM inference across GPU fleets.

long_form_blocks.heading: Overview
paragraphs: LLMKube is an Apache 2.0-licensed Kubernetes operator that turns self-hosted LLM inference into a declarative YAML deployment. It handles downloading and caching models, GPU scheduling, health checks, autoscaling, and exposing an OpenAI-compatible API, across a mix of NVIDIA CUDA, AMD Vulkan, and Apple Silicon Metal hardware.

It supports multiple inference runtimes — llama.cpp, vLLM, TGI, mlx-server, or custom containers — with autoscaling driven by real inference metrics rather than generic CPU usage, plus GPU memory optimization (layer offloading, custom sharding) and built-in Grafana dashboards for monitoring. An optional add-on called Foreman can dispatch coder, verifier, and reviewer agents across the fleet of locally hosted models.

long_form_blocks.heading: Who It's For
paragraphs: LLMKube is aimed at infrastructure and platform teams already running Kubernetes who want to scale self-hosted LLM inference beyond a single prototype box, including air-gapped or security-sensitive environments where inference must stay on-premises.

features.title: Kubernetes operator
features.description: Deploy LLM inference services declaratively via YAML instead of hand-managing pods and scaling.

features.title: Multi-runtime support
features.description: Choose llama.cpp, vLLM, TGI, mlx-server, or bring your own container.

features.title: Heterogeneous GPU support
features.description: Runs across NVIDIA CUDA, AMD Vulkan, and Apple Silicon Metal hardware in the same fleet.

features.title: Metric-driven autoscaling
features.description: Scales pods based on actual inference load rather than generic CPU/memory metrics.

features.title: GPU memory optimization
features.description: Layer offloading and custom sharding to fit larger models on available hardware.

features.title: Built-in monitoring
features.description: Grafana dashboards for inference performance out of the box.

features.title: OpenAI-compatible API
features.description: Exposes deployed models through a standard OpenAI-compatible endpoint.

pros: Fully open source under Apache 2.0
Keeps inference entirely on your own infrastructure — nothing sent to external providers
Supports mixed GPU hardware (NVIDIA, AMD, Apple Silicon) in a single deployment
Production-oriented autoscaling and monitoring, not just a single-node script
cons: Requires existing Kubernetes infrastructure and expertise to use
Young, community-driven project without an established enterprise track record
Not aimed at non-technical users or simple single-machine setups

faqs.question: Is LLMKube free?
faqs.answer: Yes, it's open source under the Apache 2.0 license.

faqs.question: Does LLMKube require Kubernetes?
faqs.answer: Yes, it's built as a Kubernetes operator and assumes an existing cluster.

faqs.question: What inference engines does it support?
faqs.answer: llama.cpp, vLLM, TGI, mlx-server, and custom inference containers.

faqs.question: Can it run on Apple Silicon?
faqs.answer: Yes, via a native Metal agent that runs llama-server on macOS with GPU access and registers back into the Kubernetes cluster.

use_cases.title: Scaled self-hosted LLM inference
use_cases.description: Move beyond a single prototype machine to production-grade, autoscaled LLM inference on Kubernetes.

use_cases.title: Mixed-hardware GPU fleets
use_cases.description: Run inference across a combination of NVIDIA, AMD, and Apple Silicon machines under one operator.

use_cases.title: Air-gapped or security-sensitive deployments
use_cases.description: Keep model inference entirely on-premises with no external API calls.

enrichment.seo_meta_description: LLMKube is an open-source Kubernetes operator for deploying, autoscaling, and monitoring self-hosted LLM inference across NVIDIA, AMD, and Apple Silicon GPUs.

enrichment.short_description: Open-source, self-hostable AI chat platform with RAG over internal documents and connectors to workplace apps.

long_form_blocks.heading: Overview
paragraphs: Onyx (formerly Danswer) is an AI platform that connects generative AI chat to a team's internal documents, applications, and knowledge. The Community Edition is MIT-licensed and free to self-host, covering the core chat, retrieval-augmented generation, agent, and action features.

Onyx combines hybrid search, advanced RAG, contextual retrieval, and LLM-based knowledge graphs with plug-and-play connectors that sync from workplace apps in real time while respecting each source's existing access permissions. It supports deep research, a code interpreter, web search, and MCP (Model Context Protocol), and can be deployed in a full-featured "standard" mode or a lighter-weight "lite" mode that runs under 1GB of memory.

long_form_blocks.heading: Who It's For
paragraphs: Onyx Community Edition suits engineering, support, and operations teams that want an internal AI assistant grounded in their own documents and tools, with the option to keep everything self-hosted for full data control. A separate managed cloud and Enterprise Edition are available for organizations that want a hosted service or additional enterprise-grade controls.

features.title: RAG-based chat
features.description: Hybrid search, advanced retrieval, and knowledge-graph techniques to answer questions from internal documents.

features.title: Deep research and code interpreter
features.description: Chat modes that go beyond simple Q&A, including running code and multi-step research.

features.title: MCP support
features.description: Connects to tools and data sources via the Model Context Protocol.

features.title: Workplace connectors
features.description: Plug-and-play connectors to workplace apps that sync in real time while respecting each source's access permissions.

features.title: Chat sharing and analytics
features.description: Share conversations, collect user feedback, and review usage analytics.

features.title: Standard and Lite deployment modes
features.description: Run the full stack or a lightweight chat UI mode that needs under 1GB of memory.

features.title: Model-agnostic
features.description: Works with any LLM the operator connects, rather than being tied to one provider.

pros: MIT-licensed Community Edition is fully free to self-host
Respects source-application permissions when indexing connected data
Large, active open-source community (20,000+ GitHub stars)
Works with any LLM rather than locking users into one provider
cons: Advanced enterprise controls are reserved for the separate Enterprise Edition or managed cloud
Self-hosting a RAG platform with multiple connectors requires meaningful infrastructure investment
Some documentation and features assume familiarity with Kubernetes/Docker deployments

faqs.question: Is Onyx Community Edition free?
faqs.answer: Yes, it's MIT-licensed and free to self-host.

faqs.question: What's the difference between Community Edition and Enterprise Edition?
faqs.answer: Enterprise Edition adds features aimed at larger organizations on top of the free Community Edition core.

faqs.question: Can Onyx connect to my company's apps?
faqs.answer: Yes, via connectors that sync documents from workplace apps while respecting their existing access permissions.

faqs.question: Does Onyx work with any LLM?
faqs.answer: Yes, it's designed to work with the LLM providers you connect rather than being tied to a single model.

enrichment.short_description: Free, open-source self-hosted groupware suite combining email, chat, forums, calendar, and wiki in one server.

long_form_blocks.heading: Overview
paragraphs: Citadel (Citadel/UX) is a self-hosted, GPL-licensed groupware and messaging suite descended from the original Citadel bulletin-board-system family of software, with a project history dating back to 1987. It's built around a "rooms" architecture, where each room can act as an email folder, discussion forum, real-time chat, mailing list, calendar, address book, or RSS reader — or several of these at once.

Citadel implements standard internet protocols — SMTP, IMAP, POP3, Sieve mail filtering, and GroupDAV for shared calendars and contacts — all with OpenSSL encryption, and it's written in C for a small resource footprint. It can be accessed through its own AJAX-style web interface (WebCit), a text/telnet BBS-style interface, or standard email/calendar clients, and supports multiple domains on a single install.

long_form_blocks.heading: Who It's For
paragraphs: Citadel suits sysadmins and organizations that want a self-hosted, all-in-one alternative to proprietary email and groupware servers (such as Microsoft Exchange), without licensing costs or feature-gated free tiers.

features.title: Rooms-based architecture
features.description: Each room can function as an email folder, forum, chat, calendar, address book, or RSS reader.

features.title: Full email server
features.description: Built-in SMTP, IMAP, POP3, and Sieve mail filtering.

features.title: GroupDAV support
features.description: Shared calendars and contacts compatible with standard PIM clients.

features.title: WebCit web interface
features.description: AJAX-style browser interface alongside a classic text/telnet BBS interface.

features.title: Multi-domain support
features.description: Serve multiple mail/web domains from a single Citadel install.

features.title: Encrypted protocols
features.description: OpenSSL encryption across all supported protocols.

features.title: Lightweight C implementation
features.description: Designed to run efficiently, with setup possible in minutes on your own hardware or in the cloud.

pros: Completely free with full functionality — not a stripped-down edition meant to upsell a paid tier
Long-running project with roots dating back to 1987
Combines email, chat, calendar, forums, and wiki-style rooms in a single self-hosted server
Lightweight C codebase suited to modest hardware
cons: Interface and overall UX feel dated compared to modern groupware products
Smaller active community than newer self-hosted collaboration tools
Setup and administration assume familiarity with mail server concepts (SMTP/IMAP/DNS)

faqs.question: Is Citadel really free?
faqs.answer: Yes, it's GPL-licensed open source software with full functionality and no paid upsell tiers.

faqs.question: Can Citadel replace Microsoft Exchange?
faqs.answer: It's positioned by its developers as a self-hosted alternative offering email, shared calendars, and groupware features.

faqs.question: What protocols does Citadel support?
faqs.answer: SMTP, IMAP, POP3, Sieve, and GroupDAV, all with OpenSSL encryption.

faqs.question: How do users access Citadel?
faqs.answer: Through its own WebCit AJAX web interface, a text/telnet BBS interface, or standard email/calendar clients.

enrichment.short_description: Open-source, local-first Slack and Notion alternative with chat, docs, and databases, self-hostable or cloud.

long_form_blocks.heading: Overview
paragraphs: Colanode is an open-source collaboration workspace that combines real-time chat, rich-text pages, and structured databases — positioned as a Slack and Notion alternative that puts data ownership back with the user. It's built local-first: data saves to the user's device first and syncs afterward using CRDTs (Conflict-free Replicated Data Types), so multiple people can edit without conflicts and the app keeps working offline.

Teams can self-host Colanode using the provided Docker Compose files or Kubernetes Helm charts (it needs PostgreSQL, Redis or Valkey, and a storage backend), or use the managed cloud, which is currently in a free beta with EU and US hosting regions. Clients include a web PWA, an Electron desktop app for macOS, Windows, and Ubuntu, and an iOS mobile app — all sharing the same underlying code.

long_form_blocks.heading: Who It's For
paragraphs: Colanode is aimed at teams that want a Slack- and Notion-style workspace but with full control over their data, either by self-hosting or by using a managed cloud they trust, and that value offline-capable, local-first software.

features.title: Real-time chat
features.description: Direct messages and channel-based conversations.

features.title: Rich-text pages and wiki
features.description: Document and note creation similar to a Notion-style wiki.

features.title: Structured databases
features.description: Custom fields with table, kanban, and calendar views.

features.title: File storage
features.description: Upload and manage files within workspaces.

features.title: Local-first sync via CRDTs
features.description: Data saves to the device first and syncs conflict-free, keeping the app usable offline.

features.title: Self-hosted or managed cloud
features.description: Deploy via Docker Compose/Kubernetes Helm charts, or use the managed cloud (currently free beta).

features.title: Cross-platform clients
features.description: Web PWA, Electron desktop app (macOS, Windows, Ubuntu), and an iOS mobile app sharing the same codebase.

pros: Open source and self-hostable for full data ownership
Local-first design keeps the app working offline and syncing without conflicts
Combines chat, docs, and databases in one workspace instead of separate tools
Native clients across web, desktop, and mobile
cons: Managed cloud pricing isn't finalized yet — it's free during the current beta
Self-hosting requires running PostgreSQL, Redis/Valkey, and a storage backend yourself
Confirmed mobile support is iOS; Android availability is not documented on official channels

faqs.question: Is Colanode free?
faqs.answer: Self-hosting is free. The managed cloud is currently free during its beta, with pricing to be announced later.

faqs.question: Can I self-host Colanode?
faqs.answer: Yes, Docker Compose files and Kubernetes Helm charts are provided for self-hosting.

faqs.question: What does "local-first" mean for Colanode?
faqs.answer: Data is saved to your device first and then synced to other devices/collaborators via CRDTs, so the app keeps working offline.

faqs.question: Is Colanode fully open source?
faqs.answer: Yes, the project is published on GitHub as open source.

enrichment.short_description: Free, open-source collaborative multimedia wall tool from La Digitale, an alternative to Padlet.

long_form_blocks.heading: Overview
paragraphs: Digipad, from the French nonprofit-style project La Digitale, lets people create collaborative multimedia walls for in-person classes, virtual sessions, or remote teamwork — text, images, video, links, and files can all be added to a shared board in real time. It's positioned as a free, open-source alternative to Padlet: no participant sign-in is required, and there's no limit on how many walls can be created.

The application is AGPLv3-licensed with source code published on Codeberg, is available in French, English, Spanish, and Italian, and is funded through donations and crowdfunding rather than a subscription model. Hosting runs in Switzerland on renewable-energy-powered data centers with a GDPR-conscious posture.

long_form_blocks.heading: Who It's For
paragraphs: Digipad is designed primarily for teachers and students needing a free Padlet-style tool for classroom or remote-learning activities, but it works equally well for any small team wanting a lightweight, no-signup collaborative content wall.

features.description: No cap on the number of pads that can be created.

features.title: Real-time collaboration
features.description: Contributions from multiple participants appear live.

features.title: Open source
features.description: AGPLv3-licensed with source code available on Codeberg.

features.title: Multi-language interface
features.description: Available in French, English, Spanish, and Italian.

pros: Completely free with no usage caps or paid tiers
No account required for participants to contribute
Open source and privacy-conscious (Swiss/GDPR-oriented hosting)
Simple, focused interface
cons: Geared toward education use cases rather than enterprise collaboration
Narrower feature set than commercial competitors like Padlet
Maintained by a small team, funded via donations rather than a company

faqs.answer: Yes, it's AGPLv3-licensed with source code on Codeberg.

faqs.answer: No, there's no limit on the number of pads/walls.

enrichment.short_description: Free, open-source tool from La Digitale for running live polls, brainstorms, quizzes, and word clouds.

long_form_blocks.heading: Overview
paragraphs: Digistorm, part of the La Digitale suite of free education tools, is an online application for running live surveys, questionnaires, collaborative brainstorming sessions, and word clouds. Participants join through a link or an access code without creating an account or sharing personal information, and each person sees only their own responses until results are revealed, which keeps input independent and uninfluenced by others.

Ideas gathered during a session can be reorganized in real time and exported as PDF. An optional free host account lets facilitators reuse the same categories across multiple sessions. The project is credited to Emmanuel Zimmert with support from the AFT-RN network, and its source is published on Codeberg and GitLab.

long_form_blocks.heading: Who It's For
paragraphs: Digistorm is built for teachers, trainers, and facilitators who need a quick, privacy-conscious way to run live brainstorms, polls, or quizzes with a group, without asking participants to sign up.

features.title: Live brainstorming sessions
features.description: Participants submit ideas that can be moved and reorganized in real time.

features.title: Polls and questionnaires
features.description: Create surveys and quizzes for a group to respond to live.

features.title: Word clouds

pros: Completely free with no paid plans
No sign-in required for participants
Privacy-conscious — doesn't collect participant personal data
Open source and self-hostable via its published source
cons: Geared toward education/training use rather than large-scale enterprise polling
Narrower feature set than dedicated commercial polling platforms
Maintained by a small volunteer-driven team

faqs.question: Is Digistorm free?
faqs.answer: Yes, there are no paid plans.

faqs.question: Do participants need an account?
faqs.answer: No, participants join via a link or access code without signing up.

faqs.question: Can sessions be reused?
faqs.answer: Yes, with an optional free host account, facilitators can reuse the same categories across sessions.

faqs.question: Is Digistorm open source?
faqs.answer: Yes, its source code is available on Codeberg and GitLab.

enrichment.seo_meta_description: Digistorm is a free, open-source tool from La Digitale for running live brainstorms, polls, quizzes, and word clouds without participant sign-up.

enrichment.short_description: Free, open-source tool from La Digitale for building collaborative multimedia walls, an evolution of Digipad.

long_form_blocks.heading: Overview
paragraphs: Digiwall is another free, open-source collaborative wall tool from La Digitale, the French project behind Digipad and Digistorm. It's described by La Digitale as an evolution of Digipad, adding new content-block formats (such as photo galleries, standalone images, and audio) on top of the same basic idea of a shared multimedia board.

Like the rest of the La Digitale suite, Digiwall is AGPLv3-licensed with its source code published on Codeberg, and it's tied into Digidrive, La Digitale's central dashboard for managing content created across its tools. It's funded through donations rather than subscriptions.

long_form_blocks.heading: Who It's For
paragraphs: Digiwall suits the same audience as Digipad — teachers, students, and small teams wanting a free, no-signup collaborative wall — for users who want its expanded media block types or who access it through Digidrive.

features.title: Collaborative multimedia walls
features.description: Build shared boards combining text, images, video, and links.

features.title: Expanded content blocks
features.description: Additional block formats such as photo galleries, standalone images, and audio, beyond what Digipad offers.

features.title: No sign-in required
features.description: Participants can contribute without creating an account.

features.title: Digidrive integration
features.description: Content created in Digiwall can be managed through La Digitale's central Digidrive dashboard.

features.title: Open source
features.description: AGPLv3-licensed with source code available on Codeberg.

pros: Completely free with no paid tiers
No account required for participants
Open source, with additional media block types compared to Digipad
Privacy-conscious, donation-funded project
cons: Functional overlap with Digipad from the same maker may cause confusion about which tool to use
Geared toward education use cases rather than enterprise collaboration
Maintained by a small volunteer-driven team

faqs.question: Is Digiwall really free?
faqs.answer: Yes, it's free and open source, funded by donations rather than subscriptions.

faqs.question: How is Digiwall different from Digipad?
faqs.answer: La Digitale describes Digiwall as an evolution of Digipad that adds new content block formats, such as photo galleries and audio.

faqs.question: Do participants need an account?
faqs.answer: No, contributors can add to a wall without signing in.

faqs.question: Is Digiwall open source?
faqs.answer: Yes, it's AGPLv3-licensed with source code on Codeberg.

use_cases.title: Classroom collaboration walls
use_cases.description: Gather multimedia contributions from students or workshop participants on a shared board.

use_cases.title: Rich-media content boards
use_cases.description: Use the expanded photo gallery and audio block types for richer collaborative content.
use_cases.audience: Educators wanting more media formats than a basic wall

use_cases.title: Free Padlet-style alternative
use_cases.description: Use as a no-cost, open-source substitute for commercial wall tools.
